questions.specification: In a text processing program, we need to count the occurrences of a specific character.

parts.content: Construct code that uses a `for` loop to iterate through a string `text` and counts how many times the character `'e'` appears.
Explain how the loop and selection statements work together in your code.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** that the `for` loop provides the mechanism to iterate over every character index in the string `text` <marks>R1</marks>
- **Explain** that the `if` statement (selection) evaluates the character at each index and conditionally increments the `count` only when the target character `'e'` is identified <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the traversal mechanism
steps.content: In algorithmic logic, we first need a way to visit every piece of data. The **for loop** provides the mechanism to iterate over every character index in the string. By starting at the first index and moving to the last, the loop ensures that no character is skipped during the process.

highlights.text: for loop provides the mechanism to iterate over every character index in the string text

steps.title: Apply the selection logic
steps.content: As the loop moves through each index, we need a way to decide if the current character matches our target. The **if statement** (selection) evaluates the character at each specific index. It acts as a filter, checking if the character is equal to our target (for example, `'e'`).

highlights.text: if statement (selection) evaluates the character at each index

steps.title: Coordinate the increment
steps.content: The loop and selection statement work together to manage the total. The program **conditionally increments** the `count` variable only when the `if` statement evaluates to true. This coordination ensures that the final count accurately represents only the identified target characters found during the full traversal of the string.

highlights.text: conditionally increments the count only when the target character 'e' is identified

steps.title: Define the Loop's Role
steps.content:
In this program, the functional role of the `for` loop is **iteration**. Its job is to provide the mechanism to systematically visit every character index in the string `text` from the beginning to the end.

This ensures that the program does not skip any data and processes the entire string sequence. This concept is covered in **Topic 4.3.5: Algorithms with Loops and Branching**.

highlights.text: for loop provides the mechanism to iterate over every character index in the string text

steps.title: Define Selection's Role
steps.content:
The functional role of the selection statement (the `if` statement) is to act as a **filter**. 

While the loop brings us to every character, the `if` statement evaluates the character at that specific index. It checks if it matches the target character (for example, $'e'$). The `count` variable is only incremented when this specific condition is met.

highlights.text: if statement (selection) evaluates the character at each index and conditionally increments the count only when the target character 'e' is identified

steps.title: Summary of Cooperation
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks, your explanation should highlight this synergy:

1.  **The Loop** handles the traversal (moving through the list).
2.  **The Selection** handles the logic (deciding which items to count).

Together, they allow the program to examine every possibility but only act on the ones that satisfy our requirements.

steps.title: Define the interaction
steps.content: In computational thinking, we often combine **loops** (repetition) and **selection** (branching) to process data structures like strings. This question asks us to explain how they coordinate to solve a specific problem: counting characters. This concept is a core part of **Topic 4.3.5: Algorithms with Loops and Branching**.

steps.title: The loop as the driver
steps.content: The `for` loop acts as the engine of the algorithm. It generates a sequence of index numbers (starting from 0 up to the length of the string minus 1), which allows the program to systematically visit every single character in the string `text`.

highlights.text: for loop provides the mechanism to iterate over every character index in the string text

steps.title: Selection as the filter
steps.content: Inside that loop, the `if` statement (selection) performs a logic check. At every step the loop takes, the `if` statement looks at the character at the current index and compares it to our target (e.g., the letter 'e'). If they match, it triggers the code to increment our `count` variable.

highlights.text: if statement (selection) evaluates the character at each index and conditionally increments the count only when the target character 'e' is identified

steps.title: Trace the execution
steps.content:
Let's trace how these work together using the word `"bee"` as our `text` and searching for `'e'`:

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text{Index (Loop)} & \text{Character} & \text{Selection (if == 'e'?)} & \text{Action} \\ \hline 0 & \text{'b'} & \text{False} & \text{Do nothing} \\ \hline 1 & \text{'e'} & \text{True} & \text{count = 1} \\ \hline 2 & \text{'e'} & \text{True} & \text{count = 2} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

As you can see, the loop ensures we check every position, while the selection ensures we only count the matches.

parts.content: Define the term 'parameter variable' in the context of a method that performs this counting task.
parts.markscheme:
- **Define** a parameter variable as a variable used to pass information into a method <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** that it is declared in the method signature and holds the value of the argument passed when the method is called <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the location and purpose
steps.content:
In Computer Science, a **parameter variable** is a variable declared within the **method header** (also known as the method signature). Its primary purpose is to act as a gateway that allows the program to pass specific information into the method so it can perform its task.

For our character counting task, the method signature might look like this:
$$public \space int \space countOccurrences(String \space text, char \space target)$$
Here, `text` and `target` are the parameter variables.

highlights.text: variable used to pass information into a method

steps.title: Explain the placeholder mechanism
steps.content:
Technically, the parameter variable acts as a **placeholder**. It does not have a fixed value when the code is written; instead, it waits to receive data. When the method is actually called (the "method call"), the value provided by the caller—known as the **argument**—is assigned to the parameter variable.

For example, if we call `countOccurrences("hello", 'l')`, the parameter variable `text` receives the value "hello" and `target` receives 'l'. This allows the method to work with different data every time it is used.

highlights.text: declared in the method signature and holds the value of the argument passed when the method is called

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks on an IB exam, you need to include both aspects:
1.  **Definition**: It is a variable used to pass data into a method (1 mark).
2.  **Technical detail**: It is declared in the method signature and holds the value of the argument passed during the call (1 mark).

This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic 4.1.1 (Procedures and Sub-Procedures)** and **Topic D.1.6 (Passing Data)** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Identify the task context
steps.content: In this scenario, we are looking at a method designed to count how many times a particular character appears in a string. To make this method flexible, it needs to receive specific data (like the text and the character to look for) from the part of the program that calls it.

highlights.text: count the occurrences of a specific character

steps.title: Define the general purpose
steps.content: A **parameter variable** is a special kind of variable used to pass information into a method. It acts as a placeholder that allows the method to work with different data values each time it is executed.

steps.title: Apply contextual definition
steps.content:
In our character counting task, the parameter variable is the specific variable that **holds the target character or the text** passed into the method. 

For example, if the method is `countChar(String text, char target)`, then `target` is the parameter variable that holds the specific character we want to process and count within the `text` variable.

steps.title: Explain declaration and values
steps.content: Technically, this variable is **declared in the method signature** (the header of the method). When the method is called, this parameter variable automatically takes on the value of the **argument** (the actual data) provided by the caller, allowing the counting logic to begin.

steps.title: Summary for marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks in an IB exam:
1.  **Define** it as a variable used to pass information into the method (A1).
2.  **State** that it is declared in the method signature and holds the value of the argument passed for processing—in this case, the specific text or character to be counted (A1).

questions.specification:
A fitness tracking application records various physical activities. Each activity is stored as a `Workout` object within a `TrainingLog`. 

To improve the user experience, the application provides a feature to organize the workout history. Users can extract specific workouts into a separate 'Focus List' based on their intensity or performance metrics.

For this system, the focus list is generated by identifying all workouts that exceed a user-defined distance threshold. When a workout is moved to the Focus List, it is removed from the main training log to prevent duplication in the primary view.

parts.content: In the design of the class `Session`, a variable `isPaused` is used to track the state of a workout. Outline **two** reasons why the `boolean` data type is the most appropriate choice for this variable.
parts.markscheme:
- The variable only requires two distinct states (e.g., the workout is either paused or it is currently running). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A boolean is more memory-efficient than other types like String or Integer as it technically requires only a single bit of information. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Construct the code for a method `filterByDistance` (part of the class `TrainingLog`), which takes an array of `Workout` objects called `history` and a `double` value `threshold` as parameters. 

The method should create a new `Workout` array of fixed size 50 and copy into it only those workouts whose distance is strictly greater than the `threshold` (unused positions in the returned array may remain `null`). These workouts should be removed from the `history` array (by setting their index to `null`) once they are copied. 

Assume the `history` array has a fixed size of 50 and that the `Workout` class has a method `getDistance()` which returns a `double`. The method should return the new array of workouts.

You may use any standard programming techniques or methods you have developed.
parts.markscheme:
<marks>Award [8 max]</marks>

<marks>1 mark</marks> for a correct method header including parameters (`Workout[]` and `double`);

<marks>1 mark</marks> for correctly initializing a new `Workout` array of size 50;

<marks>1 mark</marks> for initializing a counter variable for the new array index (e.g., `int count = 0`);

<marks>1 mark</marks> for a loop that iterates through the entire `history` array (e.g., `for` loop or `while` with correct bounds);

<marks>1 mark</marks> for a correct null check on the `history[i]` element before accessing methods;

<marks>1 mark</marks> for correctly calling `getDistance()` and comparing it against the `threshold`;

<marks>1 mark</marks> for correctly assigning the workout to the new array and incrementing the result index;

<marks>1 mark</marks> for nullifying the reference in the original `history` array and returning the new array;

**Example Method:**

```java
public Workout[] filterByDistance(Workout[] history, double threshold) {
    Workout[] focusList = new Workout[50];
    int focusIndex = 0;
    
    for (int i = 0; i < history.length; i++) {
        if (history[i] != null) {
            if (history[i].getDistance() > threshold) {
                // Copy to new array
                focusList[focusIndex] = history[i];
                focusIndex++;
                
                // Remove from original log
                history[i] = null;
            }
        }
    }
    return focusList;
}
```

parts.content:

- **Informed consent and transparency**: Users should be clearly informed what location data is collected, how it is used and retained, and whether it is shared/sold to third parties, with meaningful options to opt out. <marks>2 marks</marks>

questions.specification: A sorting algorithm needs to be implemented using selection statements.

parts.content: Construct code that finds the minimum value in an array `numbers` of integers.

steps.title: Initialize the minimum variable
steps.content:
To find the minimum value in an array, we first need a starting point. We initialize a variable (often named `min`) with the very first element of the array. This acts as our current "champion" to beat.

$$min = numbers[0];$$

This earns you the first mark! It ensures that we have a valid value to compare against from the start.

highlights.text: Initialize the min variable with the first array element

steps.title: Set up the loop
steps.content:
Next, we need to look at every other number in the array. We use a standard index-based `for` loop. Since we already used the element at index $0$ for our initial minimum, we should start the loop at index $1$ to avoid a redundant comparison.

$$for (int \ i = 1; \ i < numbers.length; \ i++)$$

Starting at $i = 1$ is a key efficiency step often looked for in IB markschemes.

highlights.text: Create a loop to iterate through the rest of the array

steps.title: Compare and update
steps.content:
Inside the loop, we check if the current element ($numbers[i]$) is smaller than our current $min$. If the condition is true, we have found a new minimum, so we update the $min$ variable.

$$\begin{aligned} &if (numbers[i] < min) \{ \\ &\quad min = numbers[i]; \\ &\} \end{aligned}$$

This logic covers the third and fourth marks: the selection statement (the `if`) and the assignment statement (the update).

highlights.text: Compare the current element with the current minimum using an if statement
Update the minimum when a smaller value is found

steps.title: Assemble the full code
Initialize the minimum variable
steps.content:
To find the minimum value, we first need a variable to store the "best candidate" found so far. We initialize `min` with the first element of the array. 

$$ \text{int min} = \text{numbers}[0]; $$

This ensures that our starting value is actually part of the data set. This step aligns with the first mark of the markscheme.

highlights.text: Initialize the min variable with the first array element (e.g., int min = numbers[0];)

steps.title: Set up the Enhanced For Loop
steps.content:
We use an **Enhanced For Loop** (also known as a for-each loop) to iterate through every integer in the `numbers` array. This is a clean way to access each element without managing an index variable.

$$ \text{for (int n : numbers)} \{ \dots \} $$

This satisfies the requirement to iterate through the array to check all possible values. Note that in an enhanced loop, `n` represents the value of the current element itself.

highlights.text: Create a loop to iterate through the rest of the array (e.g., for (int i = 1; i < numbers.length; i++))

steps.title: Compare and update
steps.content:
Inside the loop, we use a selection statement (`if`) to compare the current number `n` with our current `min`. If `n` is smaller, we update `min` to hold that new value.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{if (n < min) 	}\\ &\quad \text{min = n;} \end{aligned}$$

This logic ensures that by the time the loop finishes, `min` will hold the smallest value encountered in the entire array.

highlights.text: Compare the current element with the current minimum using an if statement (e.g., if (numbers[i] < min))
Update the minimum when a smaller value is found (e.g., min = numbers[i];)

steps.title: Final Code Construction
Initialize the minimum value
steps.content:
To find the minimum value in the array, we start by assuming the first element is the smallest. This gives us a baseline to compare against the rest of the numbers. In Java, this is done by assigning the element at index 0 to a variable called `min`.

$$min = numbers[0]$$

highlights.text: Initialize the min variable with the first array element

steps.title: Set up the manual counter
steps.content:
Since we are using a **while loop** approach, we need a manual counter variable (usually `i`) to keep track of our position in the array. Because we have already checked the element at index 0, we should start our search at index 1.

$$i = 1$$

steps.title: Implement the while loop
steps.content:
We create a `while` loop that continues as long as our counter is less than the total length of the array. This ensures we visit every integer in the collection. This logic falls under **Topic 4.3.5: Algorithms with Loops and Branching** in your syllabus.

$$\text{while } (i < numbers.length)$$

Inside the loop, we use a selection statement (`if`) to check if the current number is smaller than our current `min`. If it is, we update `min` with that smaller value.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{if } (numbers[i] < min) \{ \\ &\quad min = numbers[i]; \\ &\} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: Compare the current element with the current minimum using an if statement
Update the minimum when a smaller value is found

steps.title: Increment the counter
steps.content:
Crucially, in a while loop, we must manually increment the counter at the end of each iteration. If we forget this, the loop will run forever because the condition will never become false!

$$i = i + 1$$

steps.title: Final Code Construction

parts.content: Explain how the `if` statement is used in your code to compare values.
parts.markscheme:
- The `if` statement compares the current array element (`numbers[i]`) with the current minimum value stored in `min` <marks>1</marks>
- If the current element is less than the current minimum, the condition evaluates to true and the `min` variable is updated to this new value <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define the goal
steps.content: In a **selection sort** algorithm, the goal is to repeatedly find the smallest (minimum) value from the unsorted part of an array and move it to its correct position. To find this minimum, we need a way to check every value in the array against the smallest value we've found so far.

steps.title: Perform the comparison
steps.content:
The `if` statement acts as the "decision-maker" in each iteration of the loop. It compares the value at the current position, often represented as $numbers[i]$, with the current minimum value stored in a variable, such as $min$.

$$\text{if } (numbers[i] < min)$$

This check is the first requirement for earning a mark in the IB markscheme.

highlights.text: The if statement compares the current array element (numbers[i]) with the current minimum value stored in min

steps.title: Update the minimum
steps.content:
If the condition evaluates to **true** (meaning the current element $numbers[i]$ is actually smaller than our recorded $min$), the code inside the `if` block executes. 

This code updates the $min$ variable to hold the new, smaller value:

$$min = numbers[i]$$

By doing this, the algorithm "remembers" the lowest value encountered so far in that pass through the array.

highlights.text: If the current element is less than the current minimum, the condition evaluates to true and the min variable is updated to this new value

steps.title: Final Logic Summary
steps.content:
To secure both marks on an IB exam, your explanation should clearly state that:
1. The `if` statement **compares** the current element to the stored minimum.
2. If the current element is smaller, the **minimum is updated** to that value.

steps.title: Define the boolean condition
steps.content:
The `if` statement acts as a decision point in the algorithm. To compare values, it evaluates a **boolean condition** (an expression that is either true or false). In a sorting context, you typically compare the current element of the array, such as $numbers[i]$, against a variable that stores the smallest value found so far, often called $min$.

This uses the logic covered in **Topic 4.3.5: Algorithms with Loops and Branching**.

highlights.text: The `if` statement compares the current array element (`numbers[i]`) with the current minimum value stored in `min`

steps.title: Update the conditional flow
steps.content:
If the boolean condition evaluates to **true** (meaning the current element is smaller than the current $min$), the program's execution flow enters the `if` block. Inside this block, the $min$ variable is updated to the value of the current element. 

$$\text{if } numbers[i] < min: \dots$$ 

This ensure that as the algorithm iterates through the collection, the variable always holds the smallest value encountered, which is a fundamental part of the Selection Sort mechanics.

highlights.text: If the current element is less than the current minimum, the condition evaluates to true and the `min` variable is updated to this new value

parts.content: Define the term 'local variable' and explain its scope in the context of your code.
parts.markscheme:
- A local variable is a variable declared within a method or a specific block of code <marks>1</marks>
- In this code, `min` and `i` are local variables; their scope is limited to the block or method in which they are declared (for example, `i` is only accessible within the `for` loop) <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define 'local variable'
steps.content:
To start, we need a formal definition. A **local variable** is a variable that is declared within a specific method, procedure, or block of code (such as a loop or an `if` statement). 

This is a fundamental concept in **Topic 4.3: Introduction to programming**. Unlike global variables, which are accessible throughout the entire program, local variables are "private" to the section of code where they were created.

steps.title: Explain the concept of scope
steps.content:
The **scope** of a variable refers to the region of the program where that variable is "visible" or can be accessed. 

For a local variable, its scope is strictly limited to the block or method in which it is declared. Once the program finishes executing that specific block, the local variable is destroyed, and its memory is released.

steps.title: Provide a contextual example
steps.content:
Let's look at this in the context of a sorting algorithm (like Selection Sort). We typically use variables such as a loop counter $i$ or a variable to track the minimum value index, $min$.

- The variable $i$ is often declared within a `for` loop: its scope is limited to that loop. You cannot use $i$ outside of that specific loop.
- The variable $min$ might be declared inside a sorting method: its scope is limited to that method. It cannot be accessed by other parts of the program.

This ensures that these variables don't accidentally interfere with other parts of the code.

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks available, your explanation should include:
1. A local variable is declared within a method or block of code.
2. In a sorting algorithm, variables like $i$ and $min$ are local; their scope is limited to the specific block (e.g., the `for` loop) where they are defined.

highlights.text: Define the term 'local variable' and explain its scope in the context of your code.

questions.specification: In a banking application, there is a static method to calculate interest.

parts.content: Define the term static in the context of object-oriented programming.
parts.markscheme:
- State that static members belong to the class rather than instances of the class <marks>A1</marks>
- State that static members are shared among all instances of the class <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the core concept
steps.content: To define **static** in Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), we need to look at who "owns" the method or variable. Usually, methods belong to specific objects (instances), but a static member behaves differently. This question asks for a formal definition based on its relationship with the class.

highlights.text: Define the term static in the context of object-oriented programming.

steps.title: Define class-level ownership
steps.content:
The first part of the definition is about ownership. In OOP, a **static** member is one that belongs to the **class itself** rather than to any individual instance (object) created from that class. 

In the context of the banking application mentioned, the `calculateInterest` method is attached to the blueprint of the bank account, not to a specific person's account.

highlights.text: static method to calculate interest

steps.title: Explain the shared nature
steps.content:
The second key part of the definition is how it is used. Because it belongs to the class, a static member is **shared among all instances** of that class. 

This means that every single object created from the class uses the exact same version of the static method or variable. If you have 1,000 bank account objects, they all point to that one single `calculateInterest` logic.

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on an IB exam, your definition should clearly state both points:

1.  **Ownership:** Static members belong to the class rather than instances of the class. <marks>A1</marks>
2.  **Sharing:** Static members are shared among all instances of the class. <marks>A1</marks>

This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic D.3 (Program development)** in the CS syllabus, where understanding how objects access methods is crucial.

steps.title: Identify the task
steps.content: To define the term **static** using a comparative approach, we need to look at how it differs from standard **instance** variables and methods in Object-Oriented Programming (Topic D.3.1).

highlights.text: Define the term static

steps.title: Compare Ownership: Class vs Instance
steps.content:
In standard OOP, most members are **instance-level**. This means each object created from a class gets its own copy of that data. For example, in a banking app, every `Account` object has its own unique `balance`.

In contrast, a **static** member belongs to the **class itself** rather than to any specific instance. This means you don't even need to create an object to use a static method like the one mentioned in the question.

steps.title: Compare Data Sharing
steps.content:
Because static members belong to the class, they are **shared among all instances**. 

While instance variables hold unique values for each object, a static variable or method remains constant across the entire application. In our banking example, if the interest rate is static, every single account access the same shared value, rather than each account having its own separate rate.

steps.title: Final Definition
steps.content:
To earn full marks, your definition should clearly state these two comparative points:

1.  **Ownership:** Static members belong to the **class** rather than individual instances.
2.  **Sharing:** Static members are **shared** among all instances of that class.

steps.title: Access via Class Name
steps.content:
The primary advantage of a static method is that it can be called directly using the **class name** rather than requiring an instance (object) of the class to be created first.

In our banking example, if the method is in a class called `InterestCalculator`, you would call it as `InterestCalculator.calculateInterest()` instead of using the `new` keyword to create an object. This is known as **instance-independent access**.

This earns the first mark for identifying that no object instance is needed.

highlights.text: Identify that static methods can be called without creating an instance or object of the class

steps.title: Efficiency for Utility Methods
steps.content:
Because we don't need to instantiate an object, we eliminate the memory and processing overhead associated with object creation. 

This makes static methods perfect for **utility or helper methods** that perform a general task—like calculating interest—that doesn't depend on the specific data (state) of an individual bank account. The method simply takes inputs, performs a calculation, and returns a result.

This earns the second mark for explaining why this is useful for utility functions.

highlights.text: Explain that this is useful for utility or helper methods that do not rely on object state or instance variables

steps.title: Define the static nature
steps.content:
A static method belongs to the class itself rather than to any specific object (instance) created from that class. This means you can call the method directly using the class name without having to use the `new` keyword to create an object first.

In our banking example, you could call `InterestCalculator.calculate(1000)` instead of creating a whole `Account` object just to do one math problem.

Since calculating interest usually only requires input values (like principal, rate, and time) and doesn't need to change or access the specific 'state' (the unique data) of a bank account object, it is more efficient to define it as static.

This makes the method a 'helper' function that is globally accessible and doesn't consume memory by requiring unnecessary object creation.

steps.title: Define basic building blocks
steps.content: In programming, a **primitive data type** is a basic data type that is predefined by the programming language itself. You can think of these as the fundamental "building blocks" that come pre-packaged and ready to use without the programmer needing to define how they work.

steps.title: Explain simple, singular values
steps.content: The "primitive" nature means these types represent **simple, singular values**. Unlike complex objects or classes, they are atomic—meaning they are not composed of other data types and cannot be broken down into smaller parts.

Providing an example like $int$ or $double$ is an excellent way to support your definition.

steps.title: Define as building blocks
steps.content: A primitive data type is a basic data type that is predefined and provided by a programming language as a standard feature. In the context of **Topic 4.3 (Introduction to programming)**, we view these as the essential 'building blocks' used to construct more complex data structures.

steps.title: Explain structural composition
steps.content:
Using a **structural definition**, we identify a primitive data type by its internal composition. It is **atomic**, meaning it represents a single, simple value that cannot be broken down into smaller components or other data types. 

Unlike complex objects (which are composed of multiple variables and methods), a primitive has no internal structure that the programmer can decompose further.

steps.title: Properties of the int type
steps.content: In Java, the `int` data type is used to represent **integers**. These are whole numbers that do not contain any fractional or decimal parts. They are stored as 32-bit signed values, which is perfect for representing discrete, countable data in our calculator.

highlights.text: int

steps.title: Utility of int
steps.content:
For a calculator application, `int` would be used for operations that strictly require whole numbers. For example, it might be used to count the number of operations a user has performed or to act as an index for an array storing a history of previous results. 

You can learn more about how variables like these are used in **Topic 4.3.4: Variables, Constants, and Operators**.

steps.title: Properties of the double type
steps.content: The `double` data type is used for **floating-point numbers**. Unlike integers, `double` variables can represent values with decimal points. In Java, this is a 64-bit data type, providing high precision for scientific or financial calculations.

highlights.text: double

steps.title: Utility of double
steps.content:
In a calculator, `double` is essential for precision. It is used whenever a calculation could result in a fraction. For example, if a user performs a division, the result needs to be stored in a `double` to maintain accuracy.

$$\text{Result} = 5 / 2 = 2.5$$

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To earn all 4 marks, you must describe the technical property and a specific use case for each type:

1.  **int property**: Represents whole numbers/integers without decimals.
2.  **int use**: Used for counting operations or array indexing.
3.  **double property**: Represents floating-point numbers with decimal points.
4.  **double use**: Necessary for precise calculations or division results.

steps.title: Define the 'int' data type
steps.content:
In Java, the `int` data type is used to store **integers**, which are whole numbers. These numbers do not have a fractional or decimal part. 

In the context of the calculator application, `int` would be chosen when the user is performing operations that strictly involve whole values where decimal precision is not required.

highlights.text: int

steps.title: Identify practical uses for 'int'
steps.content: Beyond simple addition of whole numbers, `int` is essential for logic behind the scenes. For example, if the calculator app stores a history of calculations in an array, an `int` would be used for **array indexing** (accessing position $0, 1, 2...$). It is also used for **counting** the number of operations performed, as you cannot perform "half" an operation.

steps.title: Contrast with 'double' precision
steps.content:
While `int` handles whole numbers, the `double` data type is used for **floating-point numbers**. These are numbers that include a decimal point. 

A `double` provides much higher precision than an `int` because it can represent fractional values. In a calculator, this is vital for any scientific calculation or when the user inputs values like $3.14$.

highlights.text: double

steps.title: Analyze division accuracy
steps.content:
The most critical difference between these types appears during **division**. 

- If you use `int` to calculate $5 / 2$, Java performs **integer division** and throws away the remainder, resulting in $2$. 
- If you use `double`, the calculation $5.0 / 2.0$ results in the accurate value of $2.5$.

Therefore, `double` is necessary in the calculator to ensure that division results and fractional inputs remain accurate.

4.  **Use of `double`**: For precision in fractions and division results.

steps.title: Define the primary components
steps.content: In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), a method signature is the unique identification of a method within a class. It is defined as the combination of the **method's name** and its **parameter list**.

highlights.text: Define the method signature as the combination of the method's name and its parameter list.

steps.title: Analyze the parameter list
steps.content:
To fully define the signature, we must look at the structure of the parameter list. This list is characterized by three specific elements:

1.  **Number**: How many parameters are there?
2.  **Types**: What are the data types of those parameters (e.g., `String`, `int`)?
3.  **Order**: In what sequence do these types appear?

This structural definition ensures that the computer can uniquely distinguish one method from another, even if they share the same name (a concept known as method overloading).

highlights.text: State that the parameter list consists of the number, types, and order of the parameters, which uniquely identifies a method within a class.

steps.title: Review for the exam
steps.content: When answering this in an IB exam, remember that the return type (like `void` or `int`) is **not** part of the method signature. Focus only on the name and the parameters to earn both marks. This concept is covered in **Topic D.3: Program development**.

steps.title: Define the core components
steps.content:
In Object-Oriented Programming, a **method signature** serves as the unique identifier for a method. It is defined as the combination of two specific elements:
1. The **method name**.
2. The **parameter list**.

Together, these two components allow the system to recognize exactly which functionality is being called within a class like `Message`.

highlights.text: Define the method signature as the combination of the method's name and its parameter list.

steps.title: Detail the parameter list
steps.content:
To distinguish one method from another, the parameter list must be clearly defined. The signature depends on:
- The **number** of parameters.
- The **data types** of those parameters (e.g., `String`, `int`).
- The **order** in which they appear.

For example, `sendMessage(String user)` and `sendMessage(String user, int priority)` have different signatures because their parameter lists differ in number.

highlights.text: State that the parameter list consists of the number, types, and order of the parameters

steps.title: Explain unique identification
steps.content: The functional purpose of the signature is to **uniquely identify** a method within its class. This is what makes 'method overloading' possible—where multiple methods share the same name but perform different functions based on the different data they receive in their parameter lists.

highlights.text: which uniquely identifies a method within a class.

parts.content: Explain why the return type is not part of the method signature.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** that the compiler uses the method signature to distinguish between overloaded methods (methods with the same name) at compile time. <marks>R1</marks>

- **Reason** that the return type is not included because a method call may not use the returned value (e.g., the result is not assigned to a variable), which would make it impossible for the compiler to determine which method to invoke if only the return types differed. <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the method signature
steps.content: In Java and most Object-Oriented languages, a **method signature** consists of the method's name and its parameter list (the number, types, and order of arguments). The compiler uses this signature as a unique identifier to distinguish between overloaded methods—which are multiple methods that share the same name—at compile time.

highlights.text: Explain that the compiler uses the method signature to distinguish between overloaded methods (methods with the same name) at compile time.

steps.title: Identify the ambiguity issue
steps.content:
The return type is excluded from the signature because of potential **ambiguity**. When you call a method, you aren't required to assign its result to a variable. 

For example, if you have two methods in the `Message` class:
1.  `public int getLength()` 
2.  `public String getLength()` 

If a programmer simply writes the statement `getLength();`, the compiler would have no way of knowing which of the two methods was intended, as the context of the call provides no information about the expected return type.

highlights.text: Reason that the return type is not included because a method call may not use the returned value (e.g., the result is not assigned to a variable), which would make it impossible for the compiler to determine which method to invoke if only the return types differed.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content: Since the compiler must be able to uniquely identify which method to execute based solely on the call statement, and the return type can be ignored by the caller, only the name and parameters (the signature) can be used for differentiation.

parts.content: Construct a method in the `Message` class called `send` that takes a `User` object as a parameter and returns a `boolean` indicating success. You should include appropriate error handling within the method.

steps.title: Define the method header
steps.content:
First, we must construct the method header based on the requirements. The question specifies the name, the parameter type, and the return type. 

In Java, a method that is accessible and returns a truth value would look like this:

$$public\ boolean\ send(User\ user)$$

This aligns with **Topic 4.1.1 (Procedures and Sub-Procedures)**, where we define how data enters a method via parameters.

highlights.text: send
User object
boolean

steps.title: Implement the Try block
Implement the Catch block
steps.content:
If an error occurs during the sending process (such as a network timeout or a null reference), an exception is "thrown." The `catch` block intercepts this error. 

To satisfy the requirement of returning a `boolean` indicating failure, we return `false` inside the catch block.

```java
catch (Exception e) {
    return false; // Failure! Return false
}
```

highlights.text: appropriate error handling

steps.title: Final Method Construction
Define the method header
steps.content:
To start, we need to create the method signature. The question specifies that the method should be named `send`, take a `User` object as a parameter, and return a `boolean` value.

$$public\ boolean\ send(User\ user)$$

This follows the requirements for the return type and parameter specified in the prompt.

highlights.text: send
User
boolean

steps.title: Initialize the status variable
steps.content:
In the **Status Variable Approach**, we define a local boolean variable at the start of the method to track whether the operation succeeded. We usually initialize it to `false` as a safe default.

```java
boolean success = false;
```

steps.title: Implement the try-catch block
steps.content:
Since network operations or message sending can fail, we must wrap the logic in a `try-catch` block. This is a key part of **Topic 4.1.6: Exceptions** in the IB syllabus. 

Inside the `try` block, we attempt the operation and update our `success` variable. If an error occurs, the `catch` block ensures the program doesn't crash.

highlights.text: Include appropriate error handling within the method

steps.title: Assemble the final method
Define the method header
steps.content: First, we need to create the correct method signature. Based on the requirements, the method must be `public`, return a `boolean`, and accept a `User` object as a parameter.

highlights.text: Construct a correct method header: public boolean send(User user)

steps.title: Apply defensive programming
steps.content:
Before attempting to send the message, we should validate the input. This is the **defensive programming** part: we check if the `user` object is `null`. If it is, we return `false` immediately to prevent a `NullPointerException` later in the code.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{if (user == null) \{} \\ &\quad \text{return false;} \\ &\text{\}} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Implement the try-catch block
steps.content:
Next, we wrap the actual sending logic in a `try-catch` block. This ensures that if a runtime error occurs (like a connection failure), the program doesn't crash, and we can return `false` to indicate failure.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{try \{} \\ &\quad \text{// logic to send message} \\ &\quad \text{return true;} \\ &\text{\} catch (Exception e) \{ } \\ &\quad \text{return false;} \\ &\text{\}} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: Include a try-catch block or similar mechanism to handle potential exceptions

steps.title: Assemble the final method
steps.content:
Combining the validation and the error handling, here is the complete implementation. This approach fulfills all 4 marks: the header, the send logic, the boolean return, and the exception handling.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{public boolean send(User user) \{} \\ &\quad \text{if (user == null) return false;} \\ &\quad \text{try \{} \\ &\quad \quad \text{return messageService.sendTo(user, this);} \\ &\quad \text{\} catch (Exception e) \{ } \\ &\quad \quad \text{return false;} \\ &\quad \text{\}} \\ &\text{\}} \end{aligned}$$

questions.specification: An online store uses a `Product` class to represent items for sale.

parts.content: Define the terms 'accessor method' and 'mutator method'.
parts.markscheme:
- **Define** accessor method as a method used to retrieve the value of a private instance variable <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** that it is commonly referred to as a 'getter' method <marks>A1</marks>
- **Define** mutator method as a method used to modify or set the value of a private instance variable <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** that it is commonly referred to as a 'setter' method <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Define Accessor Method
steps.content:
In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), an **accessor method** is a method specifically designed to **retrieve** (or read) the value of a private instance variable. 

Because instance variables are usually kept private to maintain encapsulation, these methods provide a controlled way for other classes to see the data without being able to change it directly.

You earn the first mark by defining its role in retrieving data and the second mark by stating its common name: a **'getter'** method.

highlights.text: accessor method

steps.title: Define Mutator Method
steps.content:
A **mutator method** is a method used to **modify** or update the value of a private instance variable. 

These methods are essential because they allow the class to validate data before it is saved (for example, ensuring a product price is not negative) before actually changing the internal state of the object.

You earn the third mark by defining its role in modifying data and the fourth mark by stating its common name: a **'setter'** method.

highlights.text: mutator method

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To ensure you get all 4 marks on an IB exam, your definitions should look like this:

1.  **Accessor Method**: A method used to retrieve/get the value of a private variable. (**1 mark**)
2.  Alias: Commonly called a **'getter'**. (**1 mark**)
3.  **Mutator Method**: A method used to set/change/modify the value of a private variable. (**1 mark**)
4.  Alias: Commonly called a **'setter'**. (**1 mark**)

This terminology is covered in **Topic D.3.2 (Object Methods and Access)** of the OOP syllabus.

parts.content: Explain why accessor and mutator methods are important in object-oriented programming.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** that these methods provide controlled access to private instance variables, which supports the principle of encapsulation <marks>A1</marks>
- **Explain** that they protect data integrity by preventing unauthorized or direct modification of internal object states <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the Principle of Encapsulation
steps.content:
In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), **encapsulation** is the practice of bundling data (instance variables) and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit, or class. To maintain this, we usually set instance variables to `private`. 

This question asks about **accessor** (getter) and **mutator** (setter) methods, which serve as the only way for external code to interact with these private variables.

highlights.text: accessor and mutator methods

steps.title: Explain Controlled Access
steps.content:
The primary reason these methods are important is that they provide **controlled access** to an object's internal state. 

Instead of allowing any part of the program to directly change a `Product`'s price, the class provides a public interface (the methods). This ensures that the internal implementation remains hidden, which is a key part of **encapsulation**.

steps.title: Protecting Data Integrity
steps.content:
Secondly, these methods protect **data integrity**. By using a mutator method, the class can include logic to prevent unauthorized or invalid changes. 

For example, a `setPrice()` method in the `Product` class could check if a new price is negative before updating the variable. This prevents the object's internal state from becoming corrupted or logically incorrect.

highlights.text: Product

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this IB question, your explanation should cover these two distinct points:
1. **Encapsulation**: These methods provide a controlled public interface for private data.
2. **Data Integrity**: They prevent direct, unauthorized modification, allowing the object to validate changes to its state.

steps.title: Define controlled access
steps.content:
Accessor and mutator methods act as the "gatekeepers" for an object's data. In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), we usually make instance variables (like the price of a product) **private**. 

These methods provide **controlled access** to those variables, which supports the principle of **encapsulation**. Instead of letting any part of the program touch the data directly, we force all interactions to go through these specific methods.

highlights.text: Product

steps.title: Ensure data integrity
steps.content:
The most practical benefit of mutator methods is that they **protect data integrity**. Because a mutator is a method, we can write logic inside it to **validate** incoming data before it is saved.

For example, in our `Product` class, a mutator for the price could check if the new value is greater than zero. If someone tries to set a negative price, the mutator can block the change, preventing the object's state from becoming invalid or "corrupted."

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you need to mention two distinct points:
1. **Encapsulation:** They provide a controlled interface to private data.
2. **Data Integrity:** They allow for validation logic to prevent unauthorized or incorrect changes to the object's state.

parts.content: Construct accessor and mutator methods for the instance variable $price$ (type `double`) in the `Product` class.

steps.title: Understand the Requirements
steps.content:
To maintain **encapsulation** (as discussed in **Topic D.2.1**), instance variables like `price` are usually kept private. We use **accessors** (getters) to retrieve the value and **mutators** (setters) to modify it. 

We are asked to use **shadowing**, which means the parameter name in our mutator will be exactly the same as our instance variable name: `price`.

highlights.text: price (type double)

steps.title: Construct the Accessor Signature
steps.content:
The accessor method allows other classes to "get" the value of the price. 
- It must be `public` so it can be accessed from outside the class.
- Since the variable `price` is a `double`, the return type must also be `double`.
- By convention, we name it `getPrice()`.

$$public\ double\ getPrice()$$

highlights.text: public double getPrice()

steps.title: Complete the Accessor Body
steps.content:
Inside the accessor, we simply return the value stored in the instance variable.

$$\begin{aligned} &public\ double\ getPrice() \{ \\ &\quad return\ price; \\ &\} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: return price;

steps.title: Construct the Mutator Signature
steps.content:
The mutator method allows other classes to "set" or change the value. 
- It is `public` and has a return type of `void` because it doesn't return anything.
- Following the **shadowing** approach, we name the parameter `price`, matching the instance variable name.

$$public\ void\ setPrice(double\ price)$$

highlights.text: public void setPrice(double price)

steps.title: Apply Shadowing with 'this'
steps.content:
Because the parameter name (`price`) is the same as the instance variable name (`price`), the parameter "shadows" the variable. To tell Java we want to assign the parameter value to the instance variable, we use the `this` keyword.

$$\begin{aligned} &public\ void\ setPrice(double\ price) \{ \\ &\quad this.price = price; \\ &\} \end{aligned}$$ 

Here, `this.price` refers to the class instance variable, while `price` refers to the local parameter value.

highlights.text: this.price = price;

steps.title: Final Code Structure
steps.content:
Combining these parts gives us the full solution required for 4 marks:

$$\begin{aligned} &public\ double\ getPrice() \{ \\ &\quad return\ price; \\ &\} \\ \\ &public\ void\ setPrice(double\ price) \{ \\ &\quad this.price = price; \\ &\} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Construct the accessor method
steps.content:
An **accessor method** (also known as a getter) allows other classes to read the value of a private instance variable. 

To construct this for the variable `price`, we need:
1. A `public` access modifier.
2. A return type that matches the variable, which is `double`.
3. A standard naming convention like `getPrice`.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{public double getPrice()} \\ &\{ \\ &\quad \text{return price;} \\ &\} \end{aligned}$$

This earns the first 2 marks: one for the method signature and one for the return statement.

highlights.text: price
double

steps.title: Construct the mutator method
steps.content:
A **mutator method** (also known as a setter) is used to update the value of an instance variable. 

Following the **Unique Parameter Naming** approach, we give the input parameter a different name than the instance variable (e.g., `p` or `newPrice`). This avoids "variable shadowing," meaning we don't need to use the `this` keyword to tell the computer which variable is which.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{public void setPrice(double newPrice)} \\ &\{ \\ &\quad \text{price = newPrice;} \\ &\} \end{aligned}$$

This earns the final 2 marks: one for the method signature (including the parameter) and one for the assignment statement.

highlights.text: price
